preFix.title: Boarding a loft floor
preFix.subtitle: Lay loft boards over joists to create safe storage space
preFix.excerpt: How to lay loft boarding over ceiling joists to create safe storage space: assessing joist size and span, choosing a boarding system, cutting and fitting boards around obstructions, and working safely in a confined loft space.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. NHBC Standards. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A boarded loft floor turns an empty roof space into usable storage without structural work. The loft

support the boards; the boards provide a flat walking surface and distribute the load. The job is within reach of anyone comfortable working at height in a cramped space with basic tools. The critical limitation is joist size: older houses often have 100 x 50 mm joists that are sized for a ceiling load, not a storage load. Check the current regulations and, if the joists are under-sized, consult a structural engineer before loading the floor.

Only ever step on the joists while boarding is incomplete. Never step on the ceiling plasterboard between joists; it will not take your weight. Wear kneepads. Work in pairs when carrying sheet materials into the loft. Ensure the loft hatch is large enough for the boards before ordering.

Loft boarding panels (tongue-and-groove chipboard or dedicated loft board kits from any DIY merchant), 50 mm screws in a pozi countersunk head, a cordless drill and driver, a crosscut saw (or jigsaw for awkward cuts), a tape measure, spirit level, and carpenter's pencil. Buy a head torch; lofts are dark. Add extra insulation if the existing quilt insulation is below the recommended depth (270 mm for mineral-wool quilt at current regulations).

Measure the loft floor area you intend to board, noting the joist centres (typically 400 mm or 600 mm). Measure the hatch opening and compare against the board dimensions you plan to use; boards longer than the hatch diagonal cannot be manoeuvred in. If the hatch is too small, either order narrower cut boards or plan to cut boards to size before lifting them in.
If board edges do not fall over joists, cut
from 50 x 75 mm timber and fix them between the joists to support the unsupported edge. Nail or screw each noggin to the adjacent joists, packing so the top surface is flush with the joist tops.

Start from one side of the loft, laying the first board with the tongue facing outward. Butt the board squarely over the joists, check with the spirit level, and screw down at 300 mm centres along each joist. Use two screws per joist per board. Engage the tongue of the next board into the groove of the first, tapping it tight with a scrap block and hammer if needed. Stagger board end joints so no two adjacent boards have end joints at the same joist.

Measure and mark cuts for water tanks, pipe runs, and the hatch opening before cutting. A jigsaw makes curve cuts around pipes; a crosscut saw handles straight cuts. Do not pack boards tightly against cold water tanks; leave 75 mm clearance for access and ventilation.

attrs.fix: Lift the creaking board, check the joint is fully engaged, and re-screw with slightly longer screws if the originals are pulling out.
attrs.cause: Boards not fully seated into the tongue-and-groove joint, or screws not driven tight enough.

attrs.fix: Pack low joists with thin timber strips (packing pieces) to bring them level before boarding.
attrs.cause: Joists are not level across their tops. Common in older houses.
attrs.symptom: Board edges not aligning flat with each other
